column1:
The image attached demonstrates which of the following?

A: Greenfield filter in a normal vein
B: Chronic venous thrombosis with recanalization
C: Chronic thrombosis with Greenfield filter
D: Venous dissection with multiple intimal flaps
column2:
Answer: B. Chronic venous thrombosis with recanalization

Feedback:
Note the fibrous strands of the thrombus as it becomes denser and atrophies within the lumen. The anechoic areas are
thrombus free areas which allow some flow through the vessel.

column1:
____________________ cause primary varicose veins, while ___________________ cause secondary varicose veins.

A: elevated deep venous pressures, incompetent valves
B: incompetent valves, elevated deep venous pressures
C: incompetent valves, incompetent perforators
D: elevated deep venous pressures, elevated arterial pressures
column2:
Answer: B. incompetent valves, elevated deep venous pressures

Feedback:
Incompetent valves cause primary varicose veins, while elevated deep venous pressures in a diseased deep system (hx of DVT
or abnormal valves) cause secondary varicose veins. Primary varicose veins are much more common than secondary.

column1:
The most common complication of lower extremity DVT is:

A: pulmonary embolism
B: phlegmasia cerulea alba
C: valvular incompetence
D: phlegmasia cerulea dolens
column2:
Answer: C. valvular incompetence

Feedback:
The presence of DVT damages venous valvular structure and motion and venous insufficiency is the most common sequela see
with DVT.
Pulmonary embolism is the most critical complication of a DVT.
Phlegmasia alba dolens is an extensive iliofemoral thrombosis, usually due to stasis of uterine blood with recent pelvic surgery
or pregnancy. It presents as painful swelling and pallor of the entire leg (milk leg).
Phlegmasia cerulea dolens is an uncommon severe form of DVT which results from extensive thrombotic occlusion of the veins
of an extremity. It presents as painful "blue" swollen leg. Milk leg becomes Blue leg if untreated.

column1:
What is demonstrated by the images from a lower extremity venous Doppler exam?

A: Klippel-Trenaunay Webber Syndrome
B: AV fistula
C: incorrect gain settings on color Doppler, normal spectral waveform from the CFV
D: pseudoaneurysm
column2:
Answer: B. AV fistula

Feedback:
Note the pulsatility in the venous waveform which is consistent with the presence of arterial inflow proximally in the vessel. The
mosaic color pattern indicates turbulence at the connection with the artery.

column1:
The image is a transverse view of the distal popliteal fossa. Which of the following would not be a recommended treatment?

A: IV Heparin
B: Lovenox injection
C: No medication, just a warm compress
D: Coumadin therapy
column2:
Answer: C. No medication, just a warm compress

Feedback:
DVT and superficial thrombosis are present. DVT is always treated aggressively with protocols using IV Heparin, Lovenox
injection and Coumadin/Warfarin therapy. If only superficial thrombosis of the small saphenous vein is present, a warm
compress and an aspirin would usually be sufficient treatment.

column1:
A patient presents for an upper extremity venous Doppler evaluation due to bilateral arm and face swelling. There are also varicosities visible on the upper chest wall. These symptoms are most consistent with:

A: congestive heart failure and venous stasis
B: occlusive thrombus in the superior vena cava
C: occlusive thrombus in the internal jugular vein
D: Nutcracker syndrome
column2:
Answer: B. occlusive thrombus in the superior vena cava

Feedback:
Chest wall varices, facial and bilateral arm swelling are indicative of SVC thrombosis. IJV thrombosis can be related to facial
swelling but the arm would not be affected. CHF usually causes swelling in the legs.

column1:
A patient presents with a swollen left leg from hip to ankle and the right leg is normal. An lower extremity venous duplex exam shows compressible vessels bilaterally. Doppler demonstrates normal flow in the right leg, but there is loss of phasicity of flow and decreased augmentation of the left common femoral vein and proximal femoral vein. These findings are most suggestive of:

A: left ovarian mass or left external iliac vein thrombosis
B: right external iliac thrombosis or IVC thrombosis
C: bilateral external or internal iliac vein thrombosis
D: IVC thrombosis
column2:
Answer: A. left ovarian mass or left external iliac vein thrombosis

Feedback:
A left ovarian mass could compress the iliac vein to cause the swelling. Left external iliac vein thrombosis could also cause
unilateral swelling of the entire leg. IVC thrombosis or bilateral external iliac thrombosis would cause flow abnormalities in both
extremities. Internal iliac thrombosis will not affect the venous outflow of the legs.

column1:
A patient presents with a wet ulcer located on the medial calf just above the ankle. What vessel should be evaluated first as the most likely primary cause of the ulcer?

A: posterior tibial artery
B: posterior accessory saphenous vein
C: posterior tibial vein
D: small saphenous vein
column2:
Answer: B. posterior accessory saphenous vein

Feedback:
Insufficiency of the posterior arch vein (posterior accessory saphenous vein) and perforators is most commonly related to
venous ulcer formation. The PTV are part of the deep venous system and do not typically demonstrate reflux. The SSV courses
up the back of the calf, not near the ankle. A wet, oozing ulcer near the medial malleolus indicates a venous stasis ulcer.

column1:
Which of the following risk factors has the highest incidence of lower extremity thrombus formation?

A: Recent hip surgery
B: Polycythemia vera
C: Diabetes
D: Rotator cuff repair
column2:
Answer: A. Recent hip surgery

Feedback:
Patients who have undergone any type of lower extremity surgery are at the greatest risk of DVT. The 3 factors of Virchow's
triad are present in these patients. Usually these patients are confined to a bed for several days/weeks which leads to stasis.
There are significant limitations with using blood thinners immediately following surgery because it would prevent the healing
of the surgery and could lead to hemorrhage.

column1:
Which of the following would be contraindicated in this patient?

A: thermal ablation
B: Coumadin therapy
C: daily exercise
D: compression stockings
column2:
Answer: B. Coumadin therapy

Feedback:
Venous reflux is usually treated with compression stockings, thermal or laser ablation and lifestyle changes that increase the
venous flow to the heart. (exercise, elevating legs when sitting, avoid high heel shoes) Coumadin therapy would thin the blood
causing further dilatation of the damaged veins.

column1:
Which of the following patients has the lowest risk of upper extremity venous thrombosis?

A: central venous catheter for chemotherapy
B: hemophilia
C: recent shoulder surgery
D: polycythemia vera
column2:
Answer: B. hemophilia

Feedback:
Polycythemia vera (Hypercoagulability), central venous catheter (trauma), and recent surgery (trauma/stasis) will increase the
risk of thrombus formation.
Hemophilia refers to a condition causing abnormally thin blood and would be a contraindication to clot formation.

column1:
The most common location of a Baker cyst is:

A: Medial antecubital fossa
B: Anterior patellar space
C: Medial popliteal fossa
D: Inguinal canal
column2:
Answer: C. Medial popliteal fossa

Feedback:
The most common location of a Baker cyst is in the medial popliteal fossa. Most are an incidental finding on a lower extremity
exam.

column1:
Which of the following is an expected complication caused by a Baker cyst?

A: Aneurysm formation in the popliteal artery
B: Thrombosis of the popliteal vein due to extrinsic compression
C: Thrombosis of the small saphenous vein due to valvular insufficiency
D: Atherosclerosis formation in the popliteal artery
column2:
Answer: B. Thrombosis of the popliteal vein due to extrinsic compression

Feedback:
A Baker cyst can cause pain and swelling in the popliteal fossa. The extrinsic compression of the popliteal vein by the cyst can
lead to a continuous flow pattern, blood stasis and DVT formation. The arterial walls are muscular and can prevent the effects of
extrinsic compression on the popliteal artery.

column1:
Which of the following is a common infection that causes skin discoloration in patients with venous insufficiency?

A: staphylococcus epidermidis
B: stasis dermatitis
C: arteritis
D: staphylococcus aureus
column2:
Answer: B. stasis dermatitis

Feedback:
Stasis dermatitis is a common inflammatory skin disease. It is usually the earliest cutaneous sequela of chronic venous
insufficiency with venous hypertension and causes discoloration of the ankle and calf.

column1:
If a patient is diagnosed with a pulmonary embolism, what should be the next step in their patient care?

A: pulmonary angiography
B: IV Heparin
C: V/Q scan
D: lower extremity venous Doppler exam
column2:
Answer: B. IV Heparin

Feedback:
If the patient has been diagnosed with a PE, additional exams may or may not be required to locate the source of the embolism.
Immediate IV Heparin therapy should be administered.

column1:
Unilateral swelling of the lower extremity would be an expected clinical finding with:

A: Congestive heart failure
B: Thrombus in the IVC
C: Chronic kidney disease
D: Left popliteal DVT
column2:
Answer: D. Left popliteal DVT

Feedback:
Bilateral swelling is associated with renal disease, CHF, and IVC thrombosis. Left popliteal DVT would cause unilateral swelling of
the left leg.

column1:
A patient presents for an exam due to a history of Lemierre Syndrome. What vessels should be evaluated for associated findings?

A: Deep veins of the lower extremity
B: Bilateral radial and ulnar veins
C: Superficial veins of the lower extremity
D: Bilateral internal jugular veins
column2:
Answer: D. Bilateral internal jugular veins

Feedback:
Lemierre Syndrome refers to thrombophlebitis of the jugular veins with oropharyngeal infection, such as pharyngitis/tonsillitis.
Duplex evaluation can demonstrate thrombus within the jugular vein or other neck or facial veins.

column1:
Which of the following describes abnormal lower extremity venous flow response to distal augmentation in a superficial vein?

A: Augmentation causes increased flow toward heart followed by flow reversal >0.5sec toward the feet
B: Augmentation causes increased flow toward feet followed by flow reversal > 0.5 sec toward the heart
C: Augmentation causes increased flow toward feet followed by at least 3 seconds of flow reversal toward the feet
D: Augmentation causes increased flow toward heart followed by flow normalization
column2:
Answer: A. Augmentation causes increased flow toward heart followed by flow reversal >0.5sec toward the feet

Feedback:
<0.5 sec flow reversal with distal augmentation is considered within normal limits. Reflux is diagnosed when flow reversal
exceeds 0.5sec. >3 sec flow reversal is considered severe venous reflux. The best answer is Augmentation causes increased flow
toward heart followed by flow reversal >0.5sec toward the feet.

column1:
A 56yr old female presents with mild calf pain that is caused by dorsiflexion of the foot. The symptoms began after she returned from vacation in France. Yesterday she noticed a sudden red color change and edema, localized to the lower leg. These clinical findings are most suggestive of:

A: cellulitis
B: pseudoaneurysm
C: chronic insufficiency
D: acute DVT
column2:
Answer: D. acute DVT

Feedback:
The patient noticed the problem after a vacation in France = long airplane ride = venous stasis. The sudden onset of additional
symptoms indicates propagation of a clot in the calf.

column1:
Bilateral leg and foot edema are present. What is the most common cause for this?

A: Congestive heart failure
B: Chronic insufficiency in the superficial system
C: Chronic insufficiency in deep system
D: Lymphedema
column2:
Answer: A. Congestive heart failure

Feedback:
Cardiac and systemic problems lead to bilateral swelling of the lower extremities, including the feet. Lymphedema can cause
bilateral leg and foot swelling, but it is usually unilateral and less common than CHF. Chronic venous disease does not normally
cause swelling of the feet and is most commonly unilateral.

column1:
What is the most common cause of upper extremity DVT in the normal ambulatory patient population?

A: Paget - Schroetter Syndrome
B: shoulder surgery
C: radiation therapy treatments to the chest
D: indwelling catheters
column2:
Answer: A. Paget - Schroetter Syndrome

Feedback:
Paget - Schroetter Syndrome AKA spontaneous effort thrombosis:
Most common cause of axillary/subclavian DVT in the ambulatory patient population
Related to the presence of a cervical rib and thoracic outlet syndrome
Higher incidence in the dominant arm
Indwelling catheters are a common cause of upper extremity thrombosis in patients that are ill and require intravenous
medication/treatment.

column1:
Which of the following conditions is usually related to cardiac pulsatility in the leg veins?

A: DVT
B: Tachycardia
C: Bradycardia
D: Congestive heart failure
column2:
Answer: D. Congestive heart failure

Feedback:
As the heart moves in the chest it causes the ripple effect in the venous system. Normally, the upper IVC and Hepatic veins
demonstrate this ripple effect as flow reversal during cardiac systole. The "ripples" of cardiac pressure/motion usually dissipate
as they reach the mid abdomen area. In patients with CHF, the venous blood volume is increased throughout the system. The
cardiac pulsatility will extend further into the lower extremities due to the increased blood volume.

column1:
_______________ is the "gold standard" exam technique for diagnosis of venous disease in the lower extremities.

A: Arteriography
B: Venography
C: Doppler Ultrasound
D: MR Angiography (MRA)
column2:
Answer: C. Doppler Ultrasound

Feedback:
Doppler ultrasound has surpassed venography as the gold standard for evaluation of the veins in the lower extremities.

column1:
What is demonstrated on the longitudinal image taken from the mid- thigh?

A: chronic occlusive thrombosis of the femoral vein
B: occlusion of the deep femoral artery
C: occlusion of the superficial femoral artery
D: chronic occlusive thrombosis of the profunda femoral vein
column2:
Answer: A. chronic occlusive thrombosis of the femoral vein

Feedback:
Note the increased echogenicity of the thrombus and the contracted vein which are consistent with chronic venous thrombosis.
There is an area in the distal vein where the thrombus has contracted and hardened enough to produce posterior shadowing
on the image.

column1:
Patients with lower extremity venous insufficiency may describe pain

A: that is relieved by walking
B: that is relieved by lying down
C: that is relieved by placing legs in a dependent position
D: that is relieved by elevating the legs
column2:
Answer: D. that is relieved by elevating the legs

Feedback:
The dependent position helps blood to reach the lower legs more quickly. Walking and elevating the legs would increase the
flow toward the heart and reduce blood pooling in the calves. With venous insufficiency, walking will increase the arterial flow
into an already inefficient venous system. This can lead to increased pooling in the lower leg with swelling and pain.

column1:
Which of the following Doppler findings would indicate significant venous hypertension?

A: Phasic flow patterns in the lower extremity deep veins
B: Increased systolic velocities with decreased diastolic velocities
C: Absence of flow in the deep veins on deep expiration
D: Triphasic flow patterns in the lower extremity deep veins
column2:
Answer: D. Triphasic flow patterns in the lower extremity deep veins

Feedback:
Significant pulsatility in the deep veins of the lower extremity indicates increased venous pressures (venous HTN). CHF,
electrolyte imbalance and kidney dysfunction can lead to venous HTN. If the flow pattern normally seen in the IVC and hepatic
veins is seen in the legs, venous HTN is present.
Triphasic flow means the flow has 3 phases of movement in a single cardiac cycle. (The Doppler tracing crosses the baseline 3
times in a cardiac cycle.) The term is not limited to describing arterial flow. Continuous flow patterns in the veins can be a sign of
chronic obstruction proximally.

column1:
A patient has a prior history of popliteal DVT and currently complains of the increasing appearance of small varicosities on the calf. Which statement below best describes his condition?

A: The patient is suffering from secondary varicose vein formation.
B: The patient most likely has a recurrent acute DVT.
C: There is most likely a clot in the gastrocnemius veins of the calf.
D: There is most likely a Baker's cyst in the popliteal fossa causing extrinsic compression.
column2:
Answer: A. The patient is suffering from secondary varicose vein formation.

Feedback:
Primary varicosities form due to abnormal valves in the superficial venous system.
Secondary varicosities form if there is an associated problem in the deep system, such as previous DVT.

column1:
The images of the neck demonstrate which of the following?

A: Chronic DVT of the IJV
B: Acute DVT of the SVC
C: Acute DVT of the IJV
D: Large neck mass anterior to CCA
column2:
Answer: C. Acute DVT of the IJV

Feedback:
Note the engorged vein with mildly hyperechoic debris within the lumen. The acute thrombosis is causing complete obstruction
of the IJV. Chronic thrombosis would not present as an engorged vein and the thrombus would be excessively echogenic with
potential recanalization.
NORMAL IJV - FLATTENED, FLEXIBLE

column1:
A patient presents for a lower extremity venous Doppler due to leg pain in the groin and upper thigh after a recent car accident. After viewing these findings in the common femoral vein, what should you do next on this patient's exam?

A: perform a blood pressure on the thigh
B: evaluate the common femoral artery for signs of an AV fistula
C: evaluate the common femoral artery for a pseudoaneurysm
D: perform the color and Doppler evaluation of the superficial femoral vein to rule out thrombus
column2:
Answer: B. evaluate the common femoral artery for signs of an AV fistula

Feedback:
Note the pulsatility in the venous waveform which is consistent with the presence of arterial inflow proximally in the vessel. The
mosaic color pattern indicates turbulence at the connection with the artery.

column1:
What vessel is demonstrated as thrombosed on the image?

A: basilic vein
B: cephalic vein
C: short saphenous vein
D: great saphenous vein
column2:
Answer: D. great saphenous vein

Feedback:
Note the body marker indicator on the image along with the location of the vein just beneath the subcutaneous fat and anterior
to the muscular fascia. The lack of a corresponding artery also indicates a superficial vein.

column1:
Sclerotherapy:

A: is used to treat incompetent deep veins
B: refers to the treatment of veins using a radiofrequency catheter
C: refers to the treatment of deep venous reflux with compression stockings
D: involves the injection of saline into superficial varicosities
column2:
Answer: D. involves the injection of saline into superficial varicosities

Feedback:
Sclerotherapy refers to the injection of saline or other sclerosing agent that damages the vein and causes fibrosis to occur.
Ablation can be used to avoid surgical intervention in some cases, catheter based radiofrequency application can occlude the
vessels leading to overall atrophy of the vessels.

column1:
Virchow's triad of symptoms includes blood stasis, recent trauma and:

A: Hyperhydration
B: Hyperpigmentation
C: Anemia
D: Polycythemia vera
column2:
Answer: D. Polycythemia vera

Feedback:
Virchow's Triad:
1. Blood Stasis
2. Trauma
3. Hypercoagulability. Polycythemia vera refers to abnormally thickened blood.
Hyperpigmentation is related to chronic vascular disease. Hyperhydration and anemia would lead to thinner blood and less risk
of thrombus formation.

column1:
A patient presents for a venous insufficiency exam, but is unable to stand for the exam. How will this affect your exam?

A: Venous reflux may not be demonstrated in veins where it is actually present.
B: The PPG tracings will be increased in amplitude.
C: No change as long as they can move their feet to perform the heel raises.
D: The segmental pressures will be falsely elevated.
column2:
Answer: A. Venous reflux may not be demonstrated in veins where it is actually present.

Feedback:
Standing increases hydrostatic pressure to better demonstrate the reflux. If the patient is supine, a false negative exam could
be obtained because the augmentation response will be diminished without hydrostatic pressure. A seated patient will have
bent knees and hip joints which can inhibit/reduce the flow response to augmentation.

column1:
Which of the following are common risk factors for varicose vein formation?

A: smoking and high cholesterol
B: diabetes and smoking
C: post-phlebitic syndrome and high cholesterol
D: pregnancy and standing for long periods of time
column2:
Answer: D. pregnancy and standing for long periods of time

Feedback:
Smoking, high cholesterol and diabetes are risk factors for atherosclerotic disease. Post-phlebitic syndrome, pregnancy, and
standing for long periods of time.

column1:
This image demonstrates which of the following?

A: Chronic DVT
B: Partial DVT
C: Acute DVT
D: Superficial venous thrombosis
column2:
Answer: C. Acute DVT

Feedback:
Note the engorged vein with mildly echogenic material within the lumen. The vein appears completely obstructed by acute
thrombus.

column1:
Which of the following causes of thrombosis is associated with anatomic variations of the thoracic outlet?

A: shoulder surgery
B: radiation therapy treatments to the chest
C: indwelling catheters
D: Paget - Schroetter Syndrome
column2:
Answer: D. Paget - Schroetter Syndrome

Feedback:
Paget - Schroetter Syndrome AKA spontaneous effort thrombosis:
Most common cause of axillary/subclavian DVT in the ambulatory patient population
Related to the presence of a cervical rib and thoracic outlet syndrome
Higher incidence in the dominant arm
Indwelling catheters are a common cause of upper extremity thrombosis in patients that are ill and require intravenous
medication/treatment

column1:
A patient presents for a lower extremity venous Doppler to rule out venous insufficiency. Which vessels should be completely evaluated for the exam?

A: femoral and popliteal veins
B: great and short saphenous veins
C: iliac, femoral and great saphenous veins
D: femoral, popliteal, short saphenous and great saphenous veins
column2:
Answer: D. femoral, popliteal, short saphenous and great saphenous veins

Feedback:
The deep veins should be evaluated for thrombus before the reflux exam is performed. The deep veins should also be
evaluated for reflux along with the superficial veins.

column1:
A patient presents with a script for a "vascular ultrasound" due to a history of Paget Schroetter syndrome. What type of exam should be performed for this patient?

A: arterial PPG of the toes
B: segmental pressures on the lower extremities
C: lower extremity venous Doppler exam
D: upper extremity venous Doppler exam
column2:
Answer: D. upper extremity venous Doppler exam

Feedback:
Paget Schroetter syndrome refers to the formation of upper extremity DVT due to excessive motion. It is usually associated with
thoracic outlet syndrome.

column1:
A Greenfield filter is used to prevent which of the following?

A: DVT
B: Cardiac tamponade
C: Blue toe syndrome
D: Pulmonary embolism
column2:
Answer: D. Pulmonary embolism

Feedback:
A greenfield filter is inserted into the abdominal portion of the IVC (below the level of the renal veins) to catch embolic material
before it reaches the heart and lungs. It does not prevent the formation of DVT, but it does protect the patient from
complications related to embolism from a DVT.

column1:
You perform a bilateral venous Doppler due to bilateral swelling and pain in the legs. If both external iliac veins look like the tracing on the image, which of the following should be reported on your technologist worksheet?

A: May-Thurner syndrome is likely
B: Severe tricuspid regurgitation is suspected
C: Congestive heart failure is likely
D: IVC obstruction is suspected
column2:
Answer: D. IVC obstruction is suspected

Feedback:
Bilateral continuous waveforms from the iliac veins indicate a proximal obstruction that affects them both. An IVC obstruction is
suspected. CHF and tricuspid regurgitation cause cardiac pulsatility in both extremities. May Thurner syndrome leads to
thrombosis of the left common iliac vein. Only the left leg would demonstrate continuous flow in the external iliac vein.

column1:
The complication demonstrated on the image are most suggestive of what vascular abnormality?

A: Acute superficial vein thrombosis
B: Peripheral arterial disease
C: Acute deep vein thrombosis
D: Infrarenal aortic aneurysm
column2:
Answer: C. Acute deep vein thrombosis

Feedback:
Note the hypoechoic and anechoic areas of tissue due to increased fluid accumulation in the tissues. Peripheral edema is a sign
of DVT. The venous outflow is occluded and fluid in the veins is "pushed" into the surrounding tissues. Acute superficial
thrombosis would not typically lead to peripheral edema if the deep system is unaffected. If the superficial vein is chronically
occluded, mild edema may develop. Peripheral arterial disease causes dry skin and toe nails due to a lack of blood supply.

column1:
Significant valvular disease in the right heart usually leads to:

A: exaggerated respiratory phasicity in the portal vein
B: cardiac pulsatility in the lower extremity veins
C: tardus parvus waveforms in the carotid arteries
D: biphasic flow in the carotid arteries
column2:
Answer: B. cardiac pulsatility in the lower extremity veins

Feedback:
Significant valvular disease in the right heart usually leads to significant pulsatility in the lower extremities and portal veins.
Significant aortic disease can cause changes to the carotid artery flow.
Aortic Stenosis - turbulent flow, post-stenotic flow changes.
Aortic Regurgitation - pulsus bisferiens

column1:
Which of the following will be administered to a patient with extensive DVT to cause lysis of the clot?

A: Heparin
B: Heparin or Coumadin
C: Coumadin
D: Streptokinase
column2:
Answer: D. Streptokinase

Feedback:
Heparin, Coumadin and Warfarin are medications that reduce the viscosity of blood to prevent propagation of the clot but they
have no clot lysing properties. Streptokinase and Urokinase are used to dissolve the thrombus in cases with significant
thrombosis of the venous system.

column1:
While scanning the popliteal artery, you notice a thrombus in the popliteal vein. Which vein(s) should be evaluated next for ascending propagation of the clot?

A: GSV
B: distal femoral vein
C: ATA and tibioperoneal trunk
D: common femoral vein
column2:
Answer: B. distal femoral vein

Feedback:
If a clot is identified in the popliteal vein, the distal femoral vein should be evaluated next for propagation of the thrombus.

column1:
A patient presents with gangrene of the foot and toes. The most likely cause of these symptoms is:

A: extensive deep and superficial vein thrombosis
B: chronic occlusion of the popliteal artery
C: coarctation of the aorta
D: a 5cm aortic aneurysm with mural thrombus
column2:
Answer: A. extensive deep and superficial vein thrombosis

Feedback:
Venous gangrene usually involves the foot and all toes, while arterial gangrene usually involves one or two toes.

column1:
Which of the following is related to the appearance of Rouleaux formation on the image?

A: Thoracic outlet syndrome
B: Intracranial collateral flow
C: Raynaud Syndrome
D: DVT
column2:
Answer: D. DVT

Feedback:
Proximal to a DVT the flow may be very slow and visible on 2D imaging; the RBCs appear lined up or stacked in rolls similar to
coin rolls from your bank which is called the Rouleaux Formation

column1:
Which of the following describes symptoms of a patient with lymphedema?

A: Bilateral calf swelling with severe pitting edema
B: Unilateral non-pitting edema that is unaffected by leg elevation
C: Unilateral left leg swelling that is relieved by elevating the legs for 30 minutes
D: Warm, red calf with significant swelling
column2:
Answer: B. Unilateral non-pitting edema that is unaffected by leg elevation

Feedback:
Lymphedema does not normally cause skin discoloration, while acute DVT can be associated with warmth and redness.
Lymphedema causes non-pitting edema, DVT causes pitting edema. Elevating the leg will have no effect on the swelling, but can
reduce swelling associated with DVT. Lymphedema is typically unilateral. DVT formation can affect one or both legs.

column1:
Lower extremity ulcers are most commonly caused by __________________.

A: chronic venous disease
B: acute venous disease
C: chronic arterial disease
D: recurrent cellulitis
column2:
Answer: A. chronic venous disease

Feedback:
Lower extremity ulcers are most commonly caused by chronic venous disease. (75%)

column1:
Which of the following is a sonographic finding of acute DVT?

A: Partial compressibility of the vein
B: Valvular insufficiency
C: Engorged vein with increased echogenicity in the lumen
D: Flattened, collapsed vessel with increased echogenicity of the wall
column2:
Answer: C. Engorged vein with increased echogenicity in the lumen

Feedback:
Chronic thrombus will demonstrate as echogenic material lining a partially collapsed vein. Acute thrombus causes the vein to
grow in size and appear engorged with blood/debris.
NORMAL IJV - Flattened, flexible shape
THROMBOSED IJV - Engorged, non-compressible

column1:
Which of the following correctly describes the effects of walking on the deep venous pressure in the lower extremity in a patient with post phlebitic syndrome? Walking causes a gradual decrease in pressure during the first 1-2 minutes of exercise that quickly returns to pre-exercise

A: Walking causes a gradual decrease in pressure during the first 1-2 minutes of exercise that quickly returns to pre-exercise levels once the patient stops walking.
B: Walking causes a significant drop in deep venous pressure that takes several minutes to return to pre-exercise levels once the patient stops walking.
C: Walking has little to no effect on the deep venous pressure, only the arterial pressure is affected by exercise.
D: Walking causes an immediate, quick drop in pressure in the deep veins at the onset of exercise that quickly returns to pre- exercise levels once the patient stops walking.
column2:
Answer: D. Walking causes an immediate, quick drop in pressure in the deep veins at the onset of exercise that quickly returns to pre- exercise levels once the patient stops walking.

Feedback:
Valvular insufficiency in the deep system commonly occurs after an episode of DVT and is a key characteristic of post-phlebitic
syndrome. Normal deep veins will only allow flow toward the heart. As the leg veins empty and pressure quickly drops with
muscle contraction, the arterial inflow is the only source of blood to refill the veins and pressure rises slowly upon stopping. In
order to refill the veins and increase deep venous pressure to pre-exercise levels, the patient will need to stand for several
minutes (>5min). Venous refill time is very fast if the deep veins contain incompetent valves. The pressure will drop quickly at
the start of exercise but will quickly return to pre-exercise levels upon standing. The deep veins are refilled by arterial inflow and
retrograde flow in the veins.

column1:
Most lower extremity thrombosis begins in the ___________________________.

A: tibial veins
B: peroneal vein
C: soleal sinuses
D: femoral vein in the adductor canal
column2:
Answer: C. soleal sinuses

Feedback:
Most lower extremity thrombosis begins in the soleal sinuses and propagates into the PTA and/or peroneal veins.

column1:
A patient presents for a lower extremity venous Doppler exam due to a history of May-Thurner Syndrome. What vessel should you closely evaluate for related findings?

A: bilateral distal femoral veins
B: right iliac vein
C: bilateral popliteal veins
D: left iliac vein
column2:
Answer: D. left iliac vein

Feedback:
May - Thurner Syndrome refers to increased incidence of left DVT due to extrinsic compression by RIGHT iliac artery.

column1:
When performing an upper extremity venous Doppler evaluation for thrombosis, which of the following veins should be evaluated on the exam?

A: axillary, cephalic, basilic and brachial veins
B: subclavian, axillary, cephalic, basilic and brachial veins
C: cephalic, basilic and brachial veins
D: axillary, brachial and deep forearm veins
column2:
Answer: B. subclavian, axillary, cephalic, basilic and brachial veins

Feedback:
When performing an upper extremity venous Doppler evaluation for thrombosis, the subclavian, axillary, cephalic, basilic and
brachial veins should be evaluated on the exam. The radial and ulnar veins are not typically evaluated at length, similar to the
calf veins on a lower extremity exam.

column1:
The most common location of valvular insufficiency is:

A: great saphenous and common femoral vein junction
B: calf vein confluence
C: short saphenous vein
D: femoral and profunda femoral vein junction
column2:
Answer: A. great saphenous and common femoral vein junction

Feedback:
The most common location of valvular insufficiency is at the SFJ (junction of great saphenous and common femoral vein
junction).

column1:
Which of the following is an expected clinical finding in a patient with a venous stasis ulcer?

A: dry and crusty
B: located at the medial malleolus
C: severe pain
D: deep crater in the skin
column2:
Answer: B. located at the medial malleolus

Feedback:
Venous ulcers are usually mildly painful, oozing, shallow ulcer located at the medial malleolus.
Arterial ulcers are usually severely painful, dry and crusty, deep ulcers located at the toes or heel.

column1:
Which of the following is a sign or symptom of chronic DVT?

A: Warmth
B: Redness
C: Positive Homan's Sign
D: Brawny Discoloration
column2:
Answer: D. Brawny Discoloration

Feedback:
Hyperpigmentation refers to brawny discoloration of the distal 1/3 of the calf. It is caused by cell debris and waste accumulating
in the tissues outside the veins. Homan's sign refers to calf pain with quick dorsiflexion of the foot. This can be a sign of a DVT
with the calf pain relating to the inability to pump out the blood when the muscle is quickly contracted. Swelling, warmth and
redness are all related to venous stasis in the vessels below the area of DVT.

column1:
A patient presents for a lower extremity venous Doppler exam due to a history of Klippel - Trenaunay - Weber Syndrome. What are the expected findings on the exam?

A: extensive acute DVT causing volume overload of the superficial venous system
B: absence of the deep veins in the leg with multiple dilated varicosities present
C: extensive atherosclerosis of the arterial system causing volume overload of the superficial venous system
D: extensive chronic DVT causing volume overload of the superficial venous system
column2:
Answer: B. absence of the deep veins in the leg with multiple dilated varicosities present

Feedback:
Klippel - Trenaunay - Weber Syndrome refers to congenital absence of the deep veins which causes numerous prominent
superficial varicosities.

column1:
The most significant complication seen with DVT is _______________________.

A: Incompetent valves
B: Cardiac Tamponade
C: Varicose veins
D: Pulmonary embolism
column2:
Answer: D. Pulmonary embolism

Feedback:
Varicose vein formation is the most common complication of DVT and pulmonary embolism is the most significant complication
of DVT. A pulmonary embolism occurs when a piece of thrombus breaks off and travels to the lungs. This occludes the bronchial
artery and leads to the inability of the lungs to replace the oxygen in the blood. 15% of all cases of sudden death are PE related.

column1:
You perform a bilateral venous Doppler due to bilateral swelling and pain in the legs. Both common femoral vein waveforms look like the tracing on the image. These findings are most consistent with:

A: peripheral arterial disease
B: severe tricuspid regurgitation
C: May Thurner syndrome
D: IVC obstruction
column2:
Answer: B. severe tricuspid regurgitation

Feedback:
Cardiac pulsatility in the lower extremity veins can be related to venous HTN. This is caused by CHF, renal dysfunction and
severe tricuspid regurgitation. May Thurner syndrome only affects the left leg due to compression of the left iliac vein. IVC
obstruction would cause continuous flow in the lower extremities.

column1:
During a color evaluation of the common femoral vein, the patient is asked to perform the Valsalva maneuver. The blue color in the CFV changes to red for approximately 3 seconds before returning to blue. What do these color Doppler findings indicate?

A: The CFV has minimal venous insufficiency.
B: Superficial venous insufficiency is present.
C: There is most likely an occlusive DVT in the superficial femoral vein causing the venous turbulence proximally.
D: A significant level of insufficiency is present and should be further evaluated with PW or CW Doppler techniques.
column2:
Answer: D. A significant level of insufficiency is present and should be further evaluated with PW or CW Doppler techniques.

Feedback:
0.5 seconds or less of flow reversal is considered normal in the superficial veins. 1 sec or less of flow reversal is considered
normal in the deep veins. 3 seconds or greater of flow reversal indicates significant venous reflux.

column1:
Which of the following are common symptoms of chronic venous disease?

A: varicosities and pallor
B: pitting edema and ulceration on great toe
C: pallor and paresthesia
D: brawny discoloration and pitting edema
column2:
Answer: D. brawny discoloration and pitting edema

Feedback:
Toe ulceration, pallor and paresthesia are signs of arterial disease. Varicosities, brawny discoloration and pitting edema are
signs of chronic venous disease.

column1:
The image attached demonstrates which of the following?

A: Reflux time, post augmentation
B: Normal augmentation response time
C: Time of venous augmentation
D: Velocity of venous reflux
column2:
Answer: A. Reflux time, post augmentation

Feedback:
The flow reversal is quantified by the time reflux is present following augmentation. <0.5 sec of reflux is normal in the superficial
system with >3 sec of reflux indicates severe insufficiency.

column1:
A 65yr old female presents with left leg swelling for over one year. Mild hyperpigmentation is present in the distal calf and ankle area. These clinical findings are most suggestive of:

A: Chronic Insufficiency
B: Cellulitis
C: Acute DVT
D: Superficial Phlebitis
column2:
Answer: A. Chronic Insufficiency

Feedback:
Chronic swelling and brown discoloration are characteristics of venous stasis. This is most commonly associated with chronic
insufficiency. Acute DVT would result in acute swelling with a red discoloration.

column1:
Venous perforators with a diameter of _______________ or greater will usually demonstrate reflux.

A: 6mm
B: 2mm
C: 2cm
D: 4mm
column2:
Answer: D. 4mm

Feedback:
Normal perforator diameter is 2mm. Venous perforators with a diameter of 4mm or greater will usually demonstrate reflux.

column1:
Venous gangrene usually involves ____________________________, while arterial gangrene usually involves________________________.

A: right foot, left foot
B: all the toes and foot, just one or two toes
C: just one or two toes, all the toes and foot
D: the foot but no toes, the toes but not the foot
column2:
Answer: B. all the toes and foot, just one or two toes

Feedback:
Venous gangrene usually involves the foot and all toes, while arterial gangrene usually involves one or two toes.

column1:
Warfarin is administered to a patient:

A: to increase platelet aggregation
B: to prevent thrombus propagation
C: to assist in clot lysis and recanalization
D: to assist in atheroma lysis and recanalization
column2:
Answer: B. to prevent thrombus propagation

Feedback:
Warfarin is administered to a patient to prevent thrombus propagation and reduce platelet aggregation. Streptokinase is used
for clot lysis in phlegmasia patients. Increased platelet aggregation would cause clot propagation.

column1:
How is Heparin administered to a patient with DVT?

A: orally
B: intra-arterial
C: intravenous
D: subcutaneous injection
column2:
Answer: C. intravenous

Feedback:
DVT treatment can include 5-10 day dose of intravenous Heparin to prevent clot extension, but it does not destroy current
thrombus.

column1:
Which of the following causes flow reversal in venous perforators?

A: superficial thrombosis
B: deep vein thrombosis
C: compression stockings
D: combination of superficial and deep thrombosis
column2:
Answer: B. deep vein thrombosis

Feedback:
DVT in the deep system forces any remaining flow into areas of lower pressure, the perforators. Flow exits the deep system
through the perforators to try to "escape" into the patent superficial system.

column1:
________________________ is a nonspecific sign of DVT. It is defined as pain experienced upon quick dorsiflexion of the foot.

A: Asher Sign
B: Homan Sign
C: Allen Sign
D: Henderson Sign
column2:
Answer: B. Homan Sign

Feedback:
Homan sign is a nonspecific sign of DVT. It is defined as pain experienced upon quick dorsiflexion of the foot. Asher sign refers
to enlargement of the spleen that extends across the midline, anterior to the abdominal aorta. The Allen Test is used to
determine ulnar artery patency related to graft availability for removing the radial artery.

column1:
An acute, occlusive thrombus is identified in the femoral vein in the adductor canal. Which of the following veins will demonstrate related blood stasis?

A: great saphenous
B: small saphenous
C: common femoral
D: proximal femoral vein
column2:
Answer: B. small saphenous

Feedback:
When answering questions regarding clot propagation, consider what vessel is proximal to the clot. When answering questions
regarding distal flow changes due to a proximal clot, consider what vessel is emptying blood into the vessel with the clot.
The DVT in the femoral vein will cause a backlog of blood in the popliteal vein. The SSV empties into the popliteal vein and will
become dilated with an obstruction in the femoral vein. The proximal femoral vein will still demonstrate normal flow due to the
perforators in the thigh filling the vein with blood from the superficial system.

column1:
A varicose vein is usually:

A: a dilated lesser saphenous vein.
B: a dilated vessel of the perforator system.
C: a dilated tributary of the deep system.
D: a dilated tributary of the greater saphenous vein.
column2:
Answer: D. a dilated tributary of the greater saphenous vein.

Feedback:
The greater saphenous vein and associated tributaries are most commonly associated with varicosity formation.

column1:
A patient in the ER has just been diagnosed with acute DVT of the popliteal vein. What would be the physician's preferred treatment plan?

A: Insert a valvulotome into the popliteal vein and recanalize the lumen.
B: Perform sclerotherapy to resolve the clot.
C: Begin the use of compression stockings and perform a follow up exam in 30 days
D: Immediate IV treatment with Heparin with 90 days of Warfarin medication once released from the hospital.
column2:
Answer: D. Immediate IV treatment with Heparin with 90 days of Warfarin medication once released from the hospital.

Feedback:
Acute DVT is usually initially treated with IV Heparin followed by at least 90 days of Warfarin/Coumadin therapy once the patient
is out of the hospital.

column1:
Which of the following lower extremity vessels would most likely be occluded in a patient with Phlegmasia Alba Dolens?

A: common femoral artery
B: saphenous veins only
C: deep veins, superficial veins and common femoral artery
D: femoral vein, deep calf veins, popliteal vein
column2:
Answer: D. femoral vein, deep calf veins, popliteal vein

Feedback:
Phlegmasia Alba Dolens refers to a condition where all deep venous vessels are occluded in the extremity causing reduced
arterial inflow. The superficial veins are usually patent. The leg has a milky white appearance due to severe outflow obstruction
and markedly reducing arterial inflow. Leads to ischemia in entire extremity with severe pain.

column1:
The attached picture is most suggestive of what acute vascular disorder?

A: Congestive heart failure
B: Unilateral DVT
C: Peripheral arterial disease
D: Bilateral DVT
column2:
Answer: B. Unilateral DVT

Feedback:
Significant edema is evident in the subcutaneous tissues. This is most commonly associated with Acute DVT. Peripheral arterial
disease is a chronic disorder. CHF or bilateral DVT would cause bilateral swelling.

column1:
A patient presents 3 days after left hip surgery for a DVT evaluation. There is severe edema from the hip to the ankle and the leg has a mild cyanotic appearance. You identify acute thrombus from the common femoral vein through the calf veins. The great saphenous vein is also occluded by thrombus. What is the diagnosis for this condition?

A: Phlegmasia Cerulea Dolens
B: Acute arterial occlusion
C: Budd Chiari syndrome
D: Phlegmasia Alba Dolens
column2:
Answer: A. Phlegmasia Cerulea Dolens

Feedback:
Phlegmasia Cerulea Dolens refers to extensive DVT occluding deep system and superficial system. Arterial inflow is significantly
affected. Severe limb edema and mottled skin are noted with cyanosis due to high concentration of deoxygenated blood. Tip for
remembering the difference between Cerulea Dolens and Alba Dolens, "C"erulea causes "C"yanosis of the leg, while Alba Dolens
results in pallor.

column1:
Which of the following describes chronic lower extremity DVT?

A: echogenic vein walls
B: exaggerated respiratory phasicity
C: vein is completely non-compressible
D: pulsatile flow pattern
column2:
Answer: A. echogenic vein walls

Feedback:
Chronic thrombus formation can cause the vein walls to appear thickened and echogenic. The wall changes diminish the venous
response to respiration. It can also lead to partial compressibility of the vein and continuous flow in the vessel (loss of
respiratory phasicity).

column1:
If greater saphenous venous flow velocity is approximately 20cm/s and appears continuous, which other vessel should be evaluated for a cause for the flow abnormality?

A: internal iliac vein
B: profunda vein
C: short saphenous vein
D: femoral and popliteal vein
column2:
Answer: D. femoral and popliteal vein

Feedback:
Venous perforators normally send flow from the superficial system into the deep system. If the deep system of the
thigh/popliteal fossa has occlusive thrombus, the majority of flow in the GSV will be rerouted to the groin to enter the common
femoral vein. The increased flow will increase the velocity in the GSV and cause a continuous flow pattern.

column1:
Which of the following is a cause for deep venous HTN?

A: Aortic coarctation
B: Congestive heart failure
C: Klippel Trenaunay Webber Syndrome
D: Diffuse atherosclerotic disease
column2:
Answer: B. Congestive heart failure

Feedback:
Venous HTN is described as increased venous volume that causes fluid to leak into interstitial spaces between tissues. Related
to thrombus formation, venous insufficiency, CHF, pregnancy

column1:
What is the most common lower extremity venous disorder?

A: May Thurner syndrome
B: venous insufficiency
C: superficial thrombophlebitis
D: DVT
column2:
Answer: B. venous insufficiency

Feedback:
Venous insufficiency is the most common lower extremity venous disorder.

column1:
Proximal to a DVT in the femoral vein, the flow appears very slow and visible on 2D imaging. The red blood cells appear lined up or stacked in rolls. What is this phenomenon called?

A: May-Thurner Syndrome
B: Raynaud phenomenon
C: Phlegmasia cerulea dolens
D: Rouleaux Formation
column2:
Answer: D. Rouleaux Formation

Feedback:
Proximal to a DVT in the superficial femoral vein, the flow appears very slow and visible on 2D imaging. The red blood cells
appear lined up or stacked in rolls. This phenomenon is called Rouleaux formation.

column1:
Which of the following is the most common cause of venous stasis ulcer formation on the distal medial calf?

A: insufficiency in the posterior tibial perforators and the posterior arch vein
B: insufficiency in the proximal thigh perforators and the great saphenous vein
C: insufficiency in the paratibial perforators and the posterior arch vein
D: insufficiency in the distal thigh perforators and the great saphenous vein
column2:
Answer: A. insufficiency in the posterior tibial perforators and the posterior arch vein

Feedback:
Insufficiency in the posterior tibial perforators and the posterior arch vein most commonly leads to venous stasis ulcer
formation on the distal medial calf.

column1:
A thrombus is identified in the soleal veins of the calf. The thrombus will propagate into what calf veins first?

A: posterior tibial and peroneal
B: peroneal, posterior tibial and anterior tibial
C: anterior tibial
D: great saphenous
column2:
Answer: A. posterior tibial and peroneal

Feedback:
When answering questions regarding clot propagation, consider what vessel is proximal to the clot. When answering questions
regarding distal flow changes due to a proximal clot, consider what vessel is emptying blood into the vessel with the clot.
The soleal vessels drain into the peroneal and posterior tibial veins of the calf. A clot in the soleal veins will first extend into the
posterior tibial and peroneal veins.

column1:
It is not possible to evaluate and document valvular insufficiency with only:

A: PW Doppler evaluation
B: Plethysmography
C: Color Doppler Evaluation
D: 2D real time imaging
column2:
Answer: D. 2D real time imaging

Feedback:
The black and white 2D images are not capable of demonstrating blood flow characteristics. Venous valve movement may be
assessed in 2D but this does not help quantify valvular incompetence.

column1:
Which of the following changes in flow can be seen with a Baker cyst?

A: continuous flow in the popliteal vein
B: continuous flow in the popliteal artery
C: pulsatile flow in the superficial femoral vein
D: pulsatile flow in the popliteal vein
column2:
Answer: A. continuous flow in the popliteal vein

Feedback:
A Baker cyst can cause pain and swelling in the popliteal fossa. The extrinsic compression of the popliteal vein by the cyst can
lead to a continuous flow pattern, blood stasis and DVT formation. The arterial walls are muscular and can prevent the effects of
extrinsic compression on the popliteal artery.

column1:
Lymphedema is commonly related to:

A: Leriche syndrome
B: a history of radiation therapy or cancer
C: a pulmonary embolism
D: peripheral arterial disease
column2:
Answer: B. a history of radiation therapy or cancer

Feedback:
Lymphedema occurs due to an obstructed lymphatic system. It can be caused by many things, but is commonly seen with
infection, injury, trauma, or cancer that affects the lymphatic system. Radiation therapy or removal of lymph nodes related to
cancer can also cause lymphedema.

column1:
Which of the following cardiac abnormalities will have no effect on venous flow characteristics in the lower extremities?

A: significant tricuspid regurgitation
B: severe tricuspid stenosis
C: premature ventricular contractions
D: congestive heart failure
column2:
Answer: C. premature ventricular contractions

Feedback:
Premature ventricular contractions are a type of cardiac arrhythmia. A PVC will cause smaller ectopic contractions before a
larger compensatory contraction. This abnormality alone will not affect venous flow characteristics in the extremities. The other
choices lead to a backlog of flow in the IVC. When the IVC/iliac veins are dilated and engorged with blood, the cardiac pulsatility
is transmitted further away from the heart due to the ripple effect.

column1:
A venous insufficiency exam is performed for a patient with multiple varicosities. The deep system is found to be competent, but there is insufficiency present in the great saphenous vein. Which of the following statements regarding the findings is true?

A: Venous insufficiency is normal in the superficial veins when varicosities are present.
B: The multiple varicosities are primary varicosities.
C: The multiple varicosities are primary and secondary varicosities.
D: The multiple varicosities are secondary varicosities.
column2:
Answer: B. The multiple varicosities are primary varicosities.

Feedback:
Varicosities with superficial venous insufficiency related to deep venous system disease = secondary varicosities
Varicosities with incompetent valves causing superficial venous insufficiency and normal deep system = primary varicosities.

column1:
What is the most accurate sonographic indicator for the presence of DVT?

A: flow abnormalities found with Color Doppler
B: lack of compressibility
C: loss of augmentation response
D: flow abnormalities found with PW Doppler
column2:
Answer: B. lack of compressibility

Feedback:
The most accurate sonographic indicator for the presence of DVT is non-compressibility of the vein.

column1:
Which of the following is related to chronic DVT?

A: Swelling and erythema
B: Dry ulceration
C: Wet ulceration
D: Engorged, non-compressible vein
column2:
Answer: C. Wet ulceration

Feedback:
Ulcerations take time to form. They are a sign of chronic venous disease. Venous ulcers are and mildly painful. Arterial ulcers
more painful and dry.

column1:
Which of the following carries an increased risk of pulmonary embolism?

A: portal vein thrombosis
B: thrombus accumulation in an aortic aneurysm
C: renal vein thrombosis
D: common femoral artery thrombosis
column2:
Answer: C. renal vein thrombosis

Feedback:
A clot in the systemic venous system can break loose and travel to the lungs. The renal veins drain into the IVC. The portal
system does not directly communicate with the systemic veins, so a clot in the portal vein cannot lead to pulmonary embolism.
An embolus in the arterial system will only travel until it becomes lodged in a smaller distal artery and cause ischemia.

column1:
Which of the following statements is true regarding a partial venous obstruction?

A: May cause continuous flow
B: May cause phasic flow
C: Usually results in significant flow reversal during Valsalva maneuver
D: May cause increased volume of flow seen with distal augmentation
column2:
Answer: A. May cause continuous flow

Feedback:
Partial DVT will decrease the flexibility of the venous wall causing a more continuous/less phasic flow pattern. Insufficiency is
assessed using the Valsalva maneuver. The maneuver does not provide pertinent information related to the diagnosis of a
partial DVT.

column1:
A patient presents with intermittent left lower leg swelling for several months. The popliteal vein demonstrates a collapsed appearance with echogenic, thickened walls. These findings are most consistent with:

A: acute femoral DVT
B: chronic popliteal DVT
C: vasculitis of the popliteal vein
D: proximal venous stenosis
column2:
Answer: B. chronic popliteal DVT

Feedback:
Chronic DVT will become echogenic as it becomes more fibrous while attached to the vein wall. The fibrous material limits the
pliability of the walls leading to a semi-collapsed appearance. Acute femoral DVT would cause a dilated popliteal vein.

column1:
A patient presents with bilateral distention of the internal jugular veins and arm swelling. The lower extremities are normal on physical examination. These findings are most suggestive of:

A: Congestive heart failure
B: SVC Syndrome
C: Subclavian steal syndrome
D: Interrupted IVC
column2:
Answer: B. SVC Syndrome

Feedback:
SVC syndrome refers to the progressive obstruction of the SVC which causes venous dilatation of the upper extremities and
neck. Doppler evaluation of the bilateral internal jugular veins will demonstrate continuous flow patterns. Respiratory phasicity
and cardiac pulsatility will be lost in the bilateral IJVs due to the SVC obstruction.

column1:
The red arrows on the image indicate which of the following:

A: Temporal tap response
B: Abnormal distal augmentation response
C: Abnormal proximal augmentation response
D: Cardiac pulsatility
column2:
Answer: B. Abnormal distal augmentation response

Feedback:
The red arrows demonstrate a significant amount of flow reversal following the rush of flow toward the heart. The patient has
venous insufficiency.

column1:
Which of the following portions of the leg would be swollen with chronic insufficiency?

A: calf only
B: ankle and calf, but not the foot
C: foot and ankle, but not the calf
D: calf, foot, ankle
column2:
Answer: B. ankle and calf, but not the foot

Feedback:
Swelling of the feet normally does not occur with venous disease (systemic or cardiac disease, yes)

column1:
Which of the following carries an increased risk of developing varicose veins?

A: Oral contraceptive use
B: Wearing compression stockings
C: Sitting with legs elevated for long periods of time
D: Wearing flat shoes instead of high heels
column2:
Answer: A. Oral contraceptive use

Feedback:
Oral contraceptive use will increase the risk of developing varicose veins. Family history, pregnancy and obesity also increase
the risk. The other three choices would reduce the risk of developing varicose veins.

column1:
A clot in the gastrocnemius veins will first propagate into what vessel(s)?

A: popliteal vein
B: deep femoral vein
C: anterior tibial veins
D: peroneal veins
column2:
Answer: A. popliteal vein

Feedback:
When answering questions regarding clot propagation, consider what vessel is proximal to the clot. When answering questions
regarding distal flow changes due to a proximal clot, consider what vessel is emptying blood into the vessel with the clot.
The gastrocnemius veins empty their blood into the popliteal vein in most patients. Gastrocnemius thrombosis will first extend
into popliteal vein.

column1:
Which of the following is not an expected finding related to the abnormality displayed on the image?

A: dilated posterior arch vein
B: spontaneous flow in the distal great saphenous vein
C: dilated paratibial perforators
D: varicose veins in the calf
column2:
Answer: B. spontaneous flow in the distal great saphenous vein

Feedback:
Severe GSV reflux would most likely lead to the formation of varicosities distally. The paratibial perforators connect the GSV to
the PTVs. The GSV pressures will be increased and this will dilate the perforators. The posterior accessory saphenous vein
(posterior arch vein) empties into the GSV below the knee. If the GSV is dilated, the accessory vein will also dilate. Flow in the
superficial veins is not usually spontaneous. As the pressure in the GSV increases, there will be no flow toward the heart without
augmentation.

column1:
The mid left femoral vein appears to be occluded by hypoechoic, non- compressible thrombus. The popliteal vein is completely compressible. Which of the following Doppler waveform characteristics is expected from the popliteal vein?

A: increased pulsatility
B: normal response to distal augmentation of calf muscle
C: decreased response to distal augmentation of calf muscle
D: loss of respiratory phasicity
column2:
Answer: D. loss of respiratory phasicity

Feedback:
The flow response to the distal augmentation would most likely be decreased but NEVER augment a patient's calf when an
acute DVT is diagnosed. The best answer is loss of respiratory phasicity because continuous flow is expected with the proximal
obstruction.

column1:
Which of the following describes an abnormal response to proximal augmentation?

A: Long venous refill time
B: Flow reversal begins upon release of the augmentation maneuver
C: No flow reversal occurs with proximal augmentation, but will occur with distal augmentation
D: Flow reversal begins at the onset of the augmentation maneuver
column2:
Answer: D. Flow reversal begins at the onset of the augmentation maneuver

Feedback:
If the proximal thigh is squeezed while evaluating the distal GSV, flow reversal will occur as soon as you start to squeeze. The
Valsalva maneuver can also be used for proximal augmentation. Flow reversal begins at the start of the Valsalva strain.

column1:
Localized redness and warmth of the distal calf is most commonly associated with:

A: Acute arterial occlusion of the popliteal artery
B: Acute DVT formation in the popliteal vein
C: Cellulitis
D: Anterior compartment syndrome
column2:
Answer: C. Cellulitis

Feedback:
Redness and warmth are most commonly seen with cellulitis but can be a symptom of acute DVT.

column1:
Post-phlebitic Syndrome:

A: refers to black discoloration of the toes caused by embolism from a AAA
B: refers to valve damage in the veins after thrombus formation
C: refers to the hair loss and thickened toenails that occur with chronic DVT
D: will usually resolve within 2 weeks after treatment for DVT
column2:
Answer: B. refers to valve damage in the veins after thrombus formation

Feedback:
Post-phlebitic Syndrome refers to valve damage in the veins after thrombus formation. Varicosities form and insufficiency is
present.

column1:
A patient presents with persistent calf pain after a recent biking accident and spending a few days on bed rest. After visual inspection of the right leg, what type of vascular exam should be performed?

A: Soft tissue exam to rule out hematoma formation
B: Segmental pressure exam
C: Lower extremity arterial Doppler
D: Lower extremity venous Doppler
column2:
Answer: D. Lower extremity venous Doppler

Feedback:
The significant difference in size demonstrates fluid accumulation in the swollen leg. The recent trauma and few days of stasis
while on bed rest most likely caused a DVT to form in the leg.

column1:
Which of the following is a long term treatment for DVT?

A: venous ligation
B: Heparin
C: Lovenox
D: Warfarin
column2:
Answer: D. Warfarin

Feedback:
Treatment for Acute DVT:
Subcutaneous shot of Lovenox immediately
Heparin administered for 5-10 days to stop the progression of the clot, but does not have clot lysing properties to decrease the
clot that has already formed
After Heparin dosage completed and recanalization has occurred, Warfarin is administered to prevent future clot formation
First DVT occurrence – Warfarin prescribed for 3 months
Recurrent DVT – Warfarin prescribed for 6+ months

column1:
Which of the following correctly describes DVT in the lower extremity?

A: Recanalization of a thrombus usually occurs around 5 days after formation occurred.
B: Acute thrombus will be completely rigid upon compression.
C: Chronic thrombus will demonstrate complete coaptation of the venous walls upon compression.
D: Heparin is used to treat a DVT due to its clot lysing properties.
column2:
Answer: A. Recanalization of a thrombus usually occurs around 5 days after formation occurred.

Feedback:
Recanalization of a thrombus usually occurs around 5 days after formation occurred. Heparin is used to treat a DVT because it
thins the blood and prevents further propagation of the clot. It does not have clot lysing properties. Acute thrombus will be
somewhat pliable upon compression. Chronic thrombus will be non-pliable upon compression.

column1:
The Valsalva maneuver is most useful when trying to assess which of the following?

A: ECA/ICA differentiation
B: DVT
C: median arcuate syndrome
D: Venous insufficiency
column2:
Answer: D. Venous insufficiency

Feedback:
In the lower extremities, the Valsalva maneuver increases intra-abdominal pressure and inhibits normal venous return to the
heart. If the venous valves are incompetent, flow reversal will be seen while the maneuver is applied.

column1:
Venous ulcers are normally located on the _______________, while arterial ulcers are normally found on the ____________.

A: calf, thigh
B: heel, toes
C: medial calf/ankle, toes and heel
D: toes and heel, medial calf/ankle
column2:
Answer: C. medial calf/ankle, toes and heel

Feedback:
Venous ulcers are normally located on the distal calf, while arterial ulcers are normally found on the toes and heel.
